%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# 4.2-5 The Scaling Property

> **Section Objective:** Learn how time-scaling a signal changes its unilateral Laplace transform.

### CONCEPTS IN THIS SECTION

- Transform-pair notation
- Scaling property
- Time compression versus s-scale expansion
- Positive scaling restriction

## 1. The Scaling Rule

This section starts from a known Laplace transform pair \(x(t) \Longleftrightarrow X(s)\) and asks: what happens when the time signal becomes \(x(at)\)?

The constant \(a\) changes the **horizontal time scale** of the signal — it compresses or stretches events along the time axis — not the vertical amplitude directly.

For example, \(x(2t)\) runs through the same waveform shape in half the time. \(x(t/2)\) stretches it to take twice as long.

> **The exam move:** Transform the time-scale change into a substitution in \(X(s)\), plus one required multiplier.

## 2. Reading a from the signal

This is **transform-pair notation**: \(x(t)\) is the time-domain signal and \(X(s)\) is its unilateral Laplace transform. This is the **starting pair**, not the scaling result yet. In exam problems, this line usually means: 'you already know the transform of the original signal — now apply a property to get the transform of a modified version.'

$$x(t) \Longleftrightarrow X(s)$$

## 3. Why the book requires a>0

This is the **core scaling formula**. Every symbol matters:

- \(a\) — a **positive** scaling factor applied to the time argument
- \(x(at)\) — the time-scaled signal; if \(a > 1\) it is compressed, if \(0 < a < 1\) it is expanded
- \(X(s/a)\) — the original transform expression evaluated at \(s/a\) (replace every \(s\) with \(s/a\))
- \(\frac{1}{a}\) — a **required multiplier** that cannot be dropped

**When to use it:** whenever the time argument is multiplied by a positive constant, e.g. \(x(2t)\), \(x(3t)\), \(x(t/2)\).

**Exam trigger:** seeing \(x(2t)\), \(x(3t)\), or \(x(t/2)\) in a problem.

**Most common misuse:** replacing \(s\) by \(as\) instead of \(s/a\), or forgetting the multiplier \(1/a\) entirely.

$$x(at) \Longleftrightarrow \frac{1}{a}X\!\left(\frac{s}{a}\right), \quad a > 0$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

## 2. Reading \(a\) from the Signal

The first step is always to identify \(a\) from the time argument.

**Example 1:** \(y(t) = x(2t)\)

The time argument is \(2t\), so \(a = 2\). Apply the formula directly:

$$y(t) = x(2t) \Longleftrightarrow \frac{1}{2}X\!\left(\frac{s}{2}\right)$$

**Example 2:** \(y(t) = x(t/3)\)

Rewrite first: \(x(t/3) = x\!\left(\tfrac{1}{3}t\right)\), so \(a = \tfrac{1}{3}\). Then \(\tfrac{1}{a} = 3\) and \(\tfrac{s}{a} = 3s\):

$$y(t) = x(t/3) \Longleftrightarrow 3\,X(3s)$$

#### Warning

Do **not** treat \(x(t/3)\) as if \(a = 3\). That would give the wrong multiplier and the wrong substitution. Always rewrite \(t/3\) as \((1/3)t\) first, then read off \(a = 1/3\).

## 3. Why the Book Requires \(a > 0\)

The scaling property in this section is restricted to **positive values of \(a\)** only.

The reason is physical: if \(a\) were negative, the substitution \(t \to at\) would **reverse the signal in time**. For a causal signal (one that is zero for \(t < 0\)), time reversal produces an **anticausal signal** — one that is nonzero only for \(t < 0\).

Anticausal signals are **not permitted** in the unilateral Laplace transform setting used here, because the unilateral transform integrates only from \(t = 0\) to \(\infty\).

> **Exam-safe rule:** In this section, apply the scaling property **only for \(a > 0\)**.

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Starting pair: \(x(t) \Longleftrightarrow X(s)\); scaling gives \(x(at) \Longleftrightarrow \dfrac{1}{a}X\!\left(\dfrac{s}{a}\right)\), valid for \(a > 0\).
- When \(a > 1\): time is compressed and the s-scale is expanded; when \(0 < a < 1\): time is expanded and the s-scale is compressed.
- Restriction: \(a > 0\) only — negative \(a\) reverses time, producing an anticausal signal incompatible with the unilateral Laplace transform.
- Main trap: writing \(X(as)\) instead of \(X(s/a)\), or dropping the required multiplier \(1/a\) — both errors give a wrong answer.

*Next, we will use transform properties to connect multiplication in one domain with convolution in the other.*
